sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: A bacterial strain sensitive to Drug A was co-cultured with a donor strain carrying a plasmid encoding a membrane efflux pump. After co-culture, formerly sensitive bacteria grew in Drug A. DNase treatment did not prevent acquisition of resistance, but disrupting the conjugation pilus did. Sequencing confirmed that resistant colonies contained the plasmid and expressed the efflux pump.
questions.stem: Which mechanism best explains the acquisition of Drug A resistance?

choices.B: Vertical inheritance from a resistant parent cell only

explanations.D: Correct. Resistance required the conjugation pilus and involved plasmid transfer from a donor strain.
explanations.A: DNase did not prevent resistance, arguing against naked DNA transformation.
explanations.B: Sensitive recipient cells became resistant during co-culture, not only through inheritance from resistant parents.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Bacterial growth after antibiotic exposure and plasmid donor co-culture.

questions.stem: Why did DNase fail to prevent resistance transfer?

choices.C: The relevant DNA was protected during cell-to-cell plasmid transfer
choices.A: DNase increases mutation rate in bacterial chromosomes
choices.D: Efflux pumps are made of DNA rather than protein

explanations.C: Correct. Conjugation transfers DNA through a pilus-associated cell contact mechanism rather than relying on extracellular naked DNA.
explanations.A: DNase degrades DNA and is not described as mutagenic here.
explanations.B: Nothing indicates Drug A alters DNase.
explanations.D: Efflux pumps are proteins encoded by DNA.

questions.stem: The efflux pump most directly increases resistance by:

choices.B: Lowering intracellular concentration of Drug A

explanations.B: Correct. Efflux pumps export antibiotics, lowering intracellular drug concentration below toxic levels.
explanations.A: Antibiotics are not converted into ATP by efflux pumps.
explanations.C: Destroying ribosomes would harm bacteria rather than confer resistance.
explanations.D: The plasmid is retained and expressed in resistant colonies.

questions.stem: Which experimental condition is the best negative control for antibiotic susceptibility?

choices.A: Sensitive bacteria grown with Drug A but no donor strain
choices.B: Resistant bacteria grown without Drug A only
choices.D: Sensitive bacteria grown in rich media without labels

explanations.A: Correct. Sensitive bacteria exposed to Drug A alone show the baseline inability to grow without resistance transfer.
explanations.B: Resistant bacteria without antibiotic do not test whether Drug A suppresses sensitive cells.
explanations.C: Sequencing donors does not establish susceptibility of recipients.
explanations.D: Rich media alone does not include the selective pressure.

questions.stem: If Drug A targets translation, which bacterial structure is most likely directly affected?

explanations.D: Correct. Bacterial translation occurs on ribosomes, a common antibiotic target.
explanations.C: Most bacterial membranes do not contain cholesterol-rich domains like animal cells.

questions.passage: A respiratory virus was tested in cultured epithelial cells. Receptor-knockout cells had very low viral titers. Pretreating wild-type cells with interferon reduced but did not eliminate viral replication. A viral protease inhibitor also reduced titer and caused accumulation of uncleaved viral polyproteins. Viral RNA was detected inside wild-type cells within 30 minutes of exposure.
questions.stem: The receptor-knockout result most directly indicates that the receptor is needed for:

choices.C: Efficient viral entry into host cells
choices.D: Interferon secretion by the culture medium

explanations.C: Correct. Low titers in receptor-knockout cells indicate that receptor binding/entry is a key early step for productive infection.
explanations.A: The receptor effect is on viral infection, not only host rRNA transcription.
explanations.B: Protease cleavage is addressed by the protease inhibitor, not receptor knockout.
explanations.D: The receptor is not described as an interferon secretion factor.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Viral titer after host-cell and drug perturbations.

questions.stem: Why does interferon pretreatment reduce viral replication?

choices.B: It induces host antiviral gene expression before infection
choices.A: It serves as the viral receptor
choices.C: It digests viral RNA extracellularly as a nuclease
choices.D: It replaces host ribosomes with viral ribosomes

explanations.B: Correct. Interferons induce antiviral states by increasing expression of genes that impair viral replication.
explanations.A: Interferon is a signaling protein, not the receptor used by the virus.
explanations.C: Interferon itself is not simply an extracellular nuclease.
explanations.D: Viruses use host ribosomes; interferon does not replace them.

questions.stem: Accumulation of uncleaved viral polyproteins supports which drug mechanism?

choices.B: Blockade of host DNA polymerase alpha
choices.C: Activation of host lysosomal digestion of all proteins
choices.D: Inhibition of receptor binding before entry

explanations.A: Correct. If viral protease is inhibited, polyproteins fail to be processed into mature viral proteins.
explanations.B: Host DNA polymerase alpha is not responsible for viral polyprotein cleavage.
explanations.C: The passage describes specific uncleaved viral polyproteins, not nonspecific digestion of all proteins.
explanations.D: Receptor blockade would reduce entry, not specifically cause uncleaved polyproteins.

questions.stem: Detection of viral RNA inside wild-type cells within 30 minutes most strongly supports:

choices.A: Immediate integration into host chromosomes for all respiratory viruses
choices.B: Complete assembly of mature virions before entry
choices.C: Host translation of antibodies within minutes

explanations.D: Correct. Early intracellular viral RNA is consistent with viral entry and uncoating soon after exposure.
explanations.A: Not all respiratory viruses integrate, and integration is not shown by RNA detection.
explanations.B: Mature virions assemble after replication, not before entry into the same host cell.
explanations.C: Antibody production is not a rapid epithelial-cell translation event in this culture assay.

questions.stem: Compared with receptor knockout, interferon-treated WT cells had higher titers because:

choices.C: Some virions can still enter through intact receptors and overcome antiviral defenses
choices.A: Interferon increases viral receptor abundance in all cases
choices.D: Receptor knockout affects only late viral assembly

explanations.C: Correct. Interferon reduces replication but does not remove the entry receptor, so infection is reduced rather than nearly abolished.
explanations.A: The passage does not indicate receptor upregulation.
explanations.B: Interferon is a host cytokine, not a supplier of viral enzymes.
explanations.D: The receptor effect is early entry, not only late assembly.
